title: GitHub Review by greptile-apps[bot]
summary: The reviewer found two issues: (1) The `CONSTRAINTS` variable in `apply-constraint-overrides.sh` uses a bare filename instead of the correct `releases/${RELEASE}/upper-constraints.txt` path, causing the script to always fail at its pre-flight check even when run correctly from the repo root — the tests have the same structural mismatch. (2) The `pip_packages` list in `extra-packages.yaml` is never consumed by the build and serves only as documentation, but neither the comment nor the verification tests enforce that it stays in sync with the Dockerfile's hard-coded extras string, risking silent drift.

code_comments.body:
**`CONSTRAINTS` path doesn't match the repo layout — script always fails from repo root**

`CONSTRAINTS` is set to the bare filename `upper-constraints.txt`, but the actual file lives at `releases/${RELEASE}/upper-constraints.txt` in the repository. When the script is invoked from the repo root as documented (`./scripts/apply-constraint-overrides.sh 2025.2`), the pre-flight check on line 133 will immediately fail with:

```
Error: 'upper-constraints.txt' not found. Run this script from the repository root.
```

…even though the caller *is* at the repo root. The error message is also wrong: following the instruction to "run from repo root" will never work because `upper-constraints.txt` doesn't exist there.

The consistent fix is to derive `CONSTRAINTS` from the `$RELEASE` argument, matching the `OVERRIDES` pattern already used below:

```suggestion
RELEASE="${1:?Usage: $0 <release>}"
CONSTRAINTS="releases/${RELEASE}/upper-constraints.txt"
OVERRIDES="overrides/${RELEASE}/constraints.txt"
```

The same issue affects the tests in `tests/scripts/test_apply_constraint_overrides.sh` (e.g., lines 817–818): each test workdir places `upper-constraints.txt` directly in the temp dir root rather than under `releases/2025.2/upper-constraints.txt`. Once `CONSTRAINTS` is corrected, the test setup should be updated to create `"$workdir/releases/2025.2/upper-constraints.txt"` to stay consistent with the real repository structure.

The consistent fix is to derive `CONSTRAINTS` from the `$RELEASE` argument, matching the `OVERRIDES` pattern already used below:

Once `CONSTRAINTS` is corrected, the test setup should be updated to create `"$workdir/releases/2025.2/upper-constraints.txt"` to stay consistent with the real repository structure.

code_comments.author: greptile-apps[bot]

code_comments.body:
**`pip_packages` is a documentation-only manifest that is silently decoupled from the Dockerfile build**

The comment on lines 8–10 correctly notes that `pip_packages` is a "reference manifest" and that the Dockerfile hard-codes the combined extras string (`/tmp/keystone[ldap,memcache_pool,oauth1]`). This means `pip_packages` is **never read** by the build process — it is pure documentation.

The risk is that someone adds a new extra to `pip_packages` (e.g., `keystone[federation]`) and expects it to be installed, when in fact the Dockerfile also needs to be updated independently. The test in `verify_release_config.sh` only checks counts (3 pip items, 4 apt items), so it would pass even if `extra-packages.yaml` and the Dockerfile diverge.

Consider either:
1. Adding an explicit assertion (e.g. in `verify_release_config.sh`) that the combined extras in `extra-packages.yaml` match what's in the Dockerfile, or
2. Strengthening the comment to make the coupling explicit: e.g., "**Warning: adding entries here does NOT automatically install them. You must also update the corresponding `uv pip install` line in `images/keystone/Dockerfile`.**"

At minimum, ensuring the warning is prominent prevents silent drift as new services are added.
